title: Chapter 9: The Dark Lord's Counteroffer
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D7+D4 -->
"You have a booking."

Sam said this over motel coffee at the Desert Rose Inn — they'd come back because the old man gave them a discount for being "the only interesting thing that's happened here since the raccoon incident of 2019."
"A booking?" Chloe was polishing her crown with a napkin.
"An email. From your old company."
Chloe's hand stopped mid-polish.

"They want to meet," Sam continued. "The CEO — the dark lord. He saw the TikTok. He saw the strawberry farm. The wellness guru takedown. The Hollywood party. You have 8 million combined views across platforms."
"He wants to sue me?"
"He wants to hire you."
Chloe set down the napkin. "What?"

"He's pivoting the company. New branding initiative: 'authentic disruption.' He wants you as the face of it. Apparently 'Queen Artemis' is exactly the kind of unhinged authenticity that Gen Z consumers respond to."
Chloe stared at Sam. Sam stared back. The motel coffee maker gurgled.
"The dark lord wants to make me his court jester."
"He wants to make you his Chief Authenticity Officer. Salary: more than either of us has ever made. Equity. Creative control."

The meeting was at a rooftop bar in West Hollywood. The kind of place where the water cost $12 and the people cost more. The CEO — whose name was actually Greg, but Chloe would only ever call him Dark Lord — was already there, sitting at a table with an ocean view and a pitcher of something green that was probably a smoothie but looked like regret.
"Chloe!" Greg stood up. He was wearing a Patagonia vest over a button-down. His teeth were impossibly white. "Or should I say — Queen Artemis."
"You can say Chloe."
"Chloe, Chloe. Sit down. Can I get you a smoothie? The kale-pineapple is transformative."
"I don't need transformation. I've already transformed."
Greg's smile adjusted — the way a GPS recalculates when you miss a turn. "Of course. You've been on quite a journey."
"I've been on a quest."
"Quest, journey — same thing. The point is, you've built something. A brand. An audience. People love you."
"People love the idea of someone who tells the truth."

"Exactly!" Greg leaned forward. "And that's what we want to bottle. Truth. Authenticity. We want you to head up our new Authentic Disruption division. You'd have a team. A budget. Complete creative freedom."
"Complete creative freedom?"
"Within brand guidelines, obviously."
Chloe nodded slowly. "So creative freedom, as long as it matches what you've already decided."
"That's just good business."
"That's just censorship with a budget."
Greg's smile hit its third recalculation of the conversation. "Chloe, I'm offering you a quarter million dollars a year."
"I know."
"Plus equity."
"I know."
"And you're not taking it?"

Chloe leaned back in her chair. The sunset was doing that LA thing where it turned the whole sky into a screensaver. "Here's the thing, Greg. A week ago, you called my PowerPoint 'uninspired' and gave the credit to Derek — who, by the way, is a goblin."
"That's — HR might have opinions about that terminology —"
"Derek is a goblin. He steals ideas and hoards credit. I'm not being metaphorical. I'm being taxonomically accurate."
Sam, sitting at the bar with a $12 water, was taking notes.
"A week ago," Chloe continued, "you thought I was disposable. Now you think I'm marketable. You haven't changed your opinion of me. You've changed your opinion of my follower count."
"That's not—"

"It is. And here's the problem: you can't bottle authenticity. You can't make a Chief Authenticity Officer. The moment you put authenticity on a payroll, it stops being authentic. It becomes a product. And products are exactly what I've been liberating people from."
Greg's Patagonia vest seemed to shrink around him.
"So no," Chloe said. "I don't want your money. I don't want your equity. I don't want your brand-safe creative freedom. I have something you can't buy."
"What's that?"

"I have a best friend who packed my keyboard when she should have called an ambulance. I have a dying Honda Civic with more character than this entire building. I have a plastic crown that means more to me than your company ever will. And I have a spreadsheet."
"A spreadsheet?"
"Sam's spreadsheet. It tracks everything that matters."
Greg looked at Sam. Sam held up her phone. The spreadsheet was open. Column H was labeled "Things That Actually Matter." It had one entry: "Chloe."
Sam stared at her phone like it had revealed something she hadn't known she'd typed. "Wait. When did I write that?"
"Probably when you were updating the spreadsheet at 2 AM at the Desert Rose Inn," Chloe said. "You add things when you're too tired to lie to yourself."
Greg was still standing there, Patagonia vest and all, looking at two women who had just collectively rejected everything he represented.
"This is... not the outcome I expected," he said.
"That's because you expected a business negotiation," Chloe said. "This was an exorcism."
They walked out of the rooftop bar and into the LA evening. The city was doing its thing below them — traffic, lights, millions of people performing their lives.
"You just turned down a quarter million dollars," Sam said.
"I know."
"Because they wouldn't let you call your boss a goblin."
"Partly. Also because I don't want to be a product."
Sam thought about this. "That's either the most delusional thing you've ever said, or the most sane."
"Same thing."
They walked a block in silence. Then Sam said: "What now?"

Chloe looked at the city: freeways like arteries, lights like campfires, the whole sprawling mess of people trying to matter. "Same thing we've been doing. Liberate souls. Call out dark wizards. Sleep on beaches when the horse dies."
"Shadowfax isn't actually dead. Gus said he can fix it for eight hundred dollars."
"The horse rises again. Even better."
Sam considered this, then nodded. "Okay."
"Okay?"
"Okay. Let's go liberate more souls."
Chloe smiled. The crown caught a streetlight and threw a tiny rainbow against the side of a passing bus. "That's my Royal Treasurer."
